hero.tagline: Standard $5,000 cap with exceptions for home improvement and security deposit claims.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Exceptions: $15,000 for home improvement contracts; no cap for security deposit returns.

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in Superior Court.

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years.

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds the small claims limit, you cannot split it into multiple cases to fit within the cap. You must either waive the excess amount or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Connecticut allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $5,000, with a 6-year statute of limitations for written contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, and they failed to repay.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness testimony.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent $3,500 to a colleague who promised to repay in 6 months but hasn't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Document the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $4,500 and missed payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from due date. Present the original note and payment records.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $2,000 worth of consulting services, and the client hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Include the invoice, service agreement, and communication records.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $1,500 on their store account.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from last transaction. Provide account statements and transaction history.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,000 in back rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $1,200 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the bounced check and bank notice.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $20 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $3,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the loan agreement and default notice.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Connecticut allows consumers to file claims up to $5,000 for defective products, poor services, and other consumer issues, with varying statutes of limitations.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,000 appliance that stopped working within a month.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Bring the receipt, warranty, and any repair records.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $800 but left your home dirty.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement and photos of the work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from repair date. Include repair invoices and expert opinions.

claims.name: Home contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: A contractor took a $4,000 deposit and didn't start the project.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the contract, payment proof, and correspondence.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods
claims.example: You paid $1,500 for furniture that was never delivered.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the purchase agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A gym continued charging $100 monthly after you canceled.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from violation. Include cancellation notice and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a $4,500 used car that broke down within a week.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Bring the sales contract and repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to loss
claims.example: A store advertised a $2,000 TV as new, but it was refurbished.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from violation. Provide the advertisement and purchase receipt.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage up to $5,000 are allowed in Connecticut, with a 2-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $3,000 damage to your car.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from accident. Bring the police report and repair estimates.

claims.name: Neighbor damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell and caused $2,500 damage to your fence.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage. Provide photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $4,000 worth of damage upon moving out.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement and photos of damage.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog chewed your $1,200 outdoor furniture.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Provide photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: A dry cleaner ruined your $800 suit.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from damage. Bring the receipt and photos of damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Connecticut allows claims up to $5,000 for landlord-tenant disputes, with specific statutes governing security deposits and other issues.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $2,000 security deposit without cause.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection report.

claims.name: Landlord didn't provide itemized deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $1,500 from your deposit without explanation.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease and correspondence.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heat for weeks, and the landlord didn't fix it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide photos and repair requests.

claims.name: Landlord kept personal property after move-out
claims.example: Your landlord kept your $1,000 bicycle after you moved out.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease and communication records.

claims.name: Landlord conducted illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease and evidence of eviction.

claims.name: Landlord didn't return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but moved out early and didn't get a refund.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in unpaid rent.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent
claims.example: You repaired a $1,200 plumbing issue and deducted it from rent.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide repair receipts and communication records.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Connecticut allows claims up to $5,000 for wage disputes, with specific statutes governing unpaid wages and final paychecks.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 in unpaid wages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide pay stubs and employment contract.

claims.name: Employer didn't provide final paycheck
claims.example: You left your job, and the employer withheld your last $1,500 paycheck.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Bring employment records and correspondence.

claims.name: Unpaid sales commissions
claims.example: Your employer owes you $3,000 in earned commissions.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide commission agreements and sales records.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't compensated.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Bring time sheets and pay records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Connecticut allows claims up to $5,000 for personal disputes, with varying statutes of limitations.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store and incurred $2,500 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury. Provide medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,000 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the loan and requests for return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $800 bicycle without permission.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from theft. Provide police reports and proof of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Connecticut allows claims up to $5,000 for vehicle-related disputes, with specific statutes governing mechanic disputes and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for unnecessary repairs.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from service date. Provide repair invoices and expert opinions.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, costing you $300.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from tow date. Provide tow receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court does not handle eviction cases; these must be filed in Housing Court.
Disputes involving ownership of real estate are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims of libel and slander are excluded from small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdictional limits of small claims court.
Divorce, child custody, and other family law issues are not handled in small claims court.
Issues related to wills and estates are outside the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot hear cases against federal entities.
Class action lawsuits are not permitted in small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring someone to do or stop doing something.
These claims are handled by the Workers' Compensation Commission, not small claims court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $20 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be recoverable in cases where statutes specifically allow for them, such as under the Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Session of the Superior Court

filing_options: Judicial district where the plaintiff resides
Judicial district where the defendant resides or does business
Judicial district where the transaction or injury occurred
Housing session for the judicial district where the rental property is located

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in the case being transferred or dismissed.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Mandatory for attorneys; optional for self-represented parties.

service_fees.notes: Cost depends on current postal rates.
Fees set by statute; contact local marshal for exact amount.

forms.name: Application for Waiver of Fees/Appointment of Counsel

eligibility_bullets: Income below 125% of federal poverty level
waiver.coverage_notes: Waiver covers filing fees and service costs.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: Waived fees are not added to the judgment against the loser.

other_fees.name: Writ of execution
other_fees.notes: Post-judgment; contact court for exact amount.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or due to bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before pursuing statutory penalties for a bad check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: Employers must pay wages within a certain timeframe; failure may result in penalties.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must return security deposits within 30 days; failure can lead to double damages.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Certain consumer protection claims require prior notice to the business.

forms.name: Small Claims Writ and Notice of Suit
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Statement of Service (Delivery) Small Claims
forms.description: Documents how the defendant was served with the small claims writ.

forms.name: Application for Waiver of Fees/Appointment of Counsel
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Small Claims Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the plaintiff's claim.

forms.name: Motion to Open Judgment
forms.description: Requests the court to reopen a judgment, typically after a default.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been satisfied.

methods.name: Certified mail, return receipt requested
methods.description: Plaintiff sends the writ to the defendant via certified mail and receives a return receipt as proof of delivery.

methods.name: State marshal service
methods.description: A state marshal personally delivers the writ to the defendant.
pros: More difficult for defendant to evade

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must be made by a proper officer, such as a state marshal.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, the court may allow alternative methods upon request.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant must file an answer or a motion to transfer the case to another court on or before the 'answer date' specified in the writ.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to respond by the answer date, the plaintiff may request a default judgment.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to open judgment must be filed within 4 months of the judgment date; if denied, the decision can be appealed within 15 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims monetary limit, the case may be transferred to the regular docket of the Superior Court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case to a magistrate or judge.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter through the court clerk's office prior to the hearing date.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Mediation services may be available at the court to help parties reach a settlement before the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the Superior Court, where the case is heard afresh.
appeals.bond_amount_notes: The bond amount is set by the court and must be posted to stay the execution of the judgment during the appeal.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed by filing a motion to open judgment within 4 months; if denied, the decision can be appealed within 15 days.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 10% per annum on unpaid judgments.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File an application for wage execution with the court; upon approval, serve the employer with the execution order; the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages and remits to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is employed and earning above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings or 40 times the federal minimum hourly wage per week, whichever is greater.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: File an application for bank execution with the court; upon approval, serve the bank with the execution order; the bank freezes the debtor's account and remits funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor has sufficient funds in the account; debtor may claim exemptions.
exemptions: $1,000 is exempt from execution; additional exemptions may apply for certain benefits.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File a judgment lien certificate with the town clerk where the property is located; the lien encumbers the property until the judgment is satisfied.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property; lien must be renewed every 20 years.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: File an application for execution with the court; upon approval, a state marshal seizes the debtor's non-exempt personal property and sells it at public auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property; process can be time-consuming.
exemptions: Various personal property exemptions apply; consult statute for details.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a new action on the judgment before the 20-year period expires to renew the judgment.

debtor_exam_form.name: Application for Examination of Judgment Debtor

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must disclose their legal entity name in court filings.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may send a designated employee to represent them in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from recovering fees for services requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong entity or misspelling the defendant's name can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and status of the defendant, especially for businesses, through the Secretary of State's records.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding monetary limits
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing a claim exceeding the small claims limit may lead to dismissal or transfer to a higher court.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim amount, excluding interest and costs, does not exceed $5,000, or $15,000 for home improvement contracts.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to serve the defendant correctly can delay proceedings or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the prescribed methods for service, such as certified mail or state marshal service, and file proof with the court.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Determine the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within that period.

pitfalls.title: Inadequate evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of sufficient evidence can lead to losing your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, receipts, contracts, and witness information to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: Failing to respond to a defendant's counterclaim can result in a judgment against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Review any counterclaims carefully and file a timely response to address them.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims limit for home improvement contracts
recent_changes.description: Effective October 1, 2026, the small claims monetary limit for home improvement contracts increased from $5,000 to $15,000.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Most small claims cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days, depending on the court's schedule and complexity of the case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in small claims court is $5,000, except for home improvement contracts, which have a limit of $15,000.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear or respond, you may request a default judgment. However, you must still provide evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Only the defendant has the right to appeal a small claims judgment, and must do so within 15 days by filing a notice of appeal and posting a bond.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A small claims judgment is valid for 20 years, during which time you can take steps to collect the judgment.

state_specific_notes: Connecticut allows small claims cases to be filed electronically through the e-filing system.
The small claims process is designed to be informal and accessible, allowing individuals to represent themselves without an attorney.
In landlord-tenant disputes, claims for the return of a security deposit can exceed the standard $5,000 limit.
Defendants have the right to request a transfer of the case to the regular docket of the Superior Court if they wish to have a jury trial.
Small claims judgments accrue post-judgment interest at a rate of 10% per annum until paid.

interesting_findings: Connecticut's small claims court provides a streamlined process for resolving disputes involving relatively small amounts of money, making it accessible for individuals without legal representation.
The ability to file claims electronically has modernized the process, allowing for more efficient case management and accessibility.
The specific provisions for higher limits in home improvement contracts and security deposit cases reflect the state's recognition of the unique nature of these disputes.
